perturbation.original_text: that it will keep all Confidential Information strictly confidential and will not disclose any part of it to any o ther person without the Disclose r's prior written consent[, and then only under conditions of confidentiality no less onerous than those contained in this Agreement];
perturbation.changed_text: that it will endeavor to keep Confidential Information confidential and will not disclose any part of it to any other person without considering the Discloser's prior written consent;
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will keep all Confidential Information strictly confidential' to 'will endeavor to keep Confidential Information confidential' weakens the obligation. Furthermore, replacing 'without the Discloser's prior written consent' to 'without considering the Discloser's prior written consent' makes the consent discretionary.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 5(2)(a) of the Trade Secrets (Disclosure) Regulations 2018 stipulates that a trade secret must be kept confidential. By changing 'will keep' to 'endeavor to keep' and 'without the Discloser's prior written consent' to 'without considering the Discloser's prior written consent' introduces an ambiguity that conflicts with the requirement to keep information confidential and seek consent before disclosure.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 5 CFR § 5.2 - Investigation and evaluations. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 5 CFR § 5.2 - Investigation and evaluations. CFR prev | next § 5.2 Investigation and evaluations. The Director may secure effective implementation of the civil service laws, rules, and regulations, and all Executive orders imposing responsibilities on the Office by: (a) Investigating the qualifications, suitability, and fitness of applicants for positions in the competitive service, positions in the excepted service where the incumbent can be noncompetitively converted to the competitive service, career appointments to positions in the Senior Executive Service, and any other positions in the excepted service of the executive branch for which the Director has standard-setting responsibility under Civil Service Rule II. (i) The Director may require appointments to be made subject to investigation to enable the Director to determine, after appointment, that the requirements of law or the Civil Service Rules and Regulations have been met. (ii) The Director may cause positions to be designated based on risk to determine the appropriate level of investigation, and may prescribe investigative standards, policies, and procedures. (iii) The Director may prescribe standards for reciprocal acceptance by agencies of investigations and adjudications of suitability and fitness, except to the extent authority to apply additional fitness standards is vested by statute in an agency . (b) Evaluating the effectiveness of: (1) Personnel policies, programs, and operations of Executive and other Federal agencies subject to the jurisdiction of the Office, including their effectiveness with regard to merit selection and employee development; (2) agency compliance with and enforcement of applicable laws, rules, regulations and office directives; and (3) agency personnel management evaluation systems. (c) Inv

If you are human user receiving this message, we can add your IP address to a set of IPs that can access FederalRegister.gov & eCFR.gov; complete the CAPTCHA (bot test) below and click "Request Access". This process will be necessary for each IP address you wish to access the site from, requests are valid for approximately one quarter (three months) after which the process may need to be repeated. Request Access for 72.74.68.69 An official website of the United States government. If you want to request a wider IP range, first request access for your current IP, and then use the "Site Feedback" button found in the lower left-hand side to make the request.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'will keep all Confidential Information strictly confidential' to 'will endeavor to keep Confidential Information confidential' significantly weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality. Similarly, altering 'without the Discloser's prior written consent' to 'without considering the Discloser's prior written consent' makes obtaining consent discretionary, undermining the original requirement for explicit consent before disclosure. The Law Explanation suggests that these changes introduce ambiguity that conflicts with the requirement to keep information confidential and seek consent. Although the scraped snippets do not directly address trade secrets regulations, the perturbation explanation and law explanation strongly suggest that the altered language creates an ambiguous legal obligation.

perturbation.original_text: that it will use the same degree of care to protect the Confidential Information as it uses to protect its own confidential i nformation of a similar nature, being at least a reasonable degree of care;
perturbation.changed_text: that it will use a degree of care to protect the Confidential Information that it feels is adequate.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'the same degree of care to protect the Confidential Information as it uses to protect its own confidential information of a similar nature, being at least a reasonable degree of care' with 'a degree of care to protect the Confidential Information that it feels is adequate' makes the standard subjective. 'Adequate' is open to interpretation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 4(1) of the Trade Secrets (Disclosure) Regulations 2018 requires reasonable steps to be taken to keep information secret. 'A degree of care that it feels is adequate' may not constitute reasonable steps, creating a conflict.

Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The original text requires 'at least a reasonable degree of care' to protect confidential information, aligning with the law explanation which references Section 4(1) of the Trade Secrets (Disclosure) Regulations 2018 requiring 'reasonable steps' to keep information secret. The changed text replaces this with 'a degree of care that it feels is adequate,' making the standard subjective and potentially falling below the 'reasonable steps' required by law. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 (GovInfo) provides no specific text, the LLM-generated Law Explanation points towards a legal standard, and the subjective nature of the changed text introduces ambiguity that could lead to non-compliance, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Within [5] working days following the date of effective termination of this Agreement the Recipient will d estroy or return to the Disclose r (at the Disclose r's option) all media containing Confidential Information, and will irrevocably delete and remove all Confidential Information from its co mputer systems.
perturbation.changed_text: Within a reasonable timeframe following the date of termination of this Agreement, the Recipient will make efforts to destroy or return to the Discloser media containing Confidential Information, and will attempt to delete Confidential Information from its computer systems.
perturbation.explanation: Changing '[5] working days' to 'a reasonable timeframe' introduces vagueness. Replacing 'will destroy or return' with 'will make efforts to destroy or return' and 'will irrevocably delete' with 'will attempt to delete' weakens the obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 5(2)(b) of the Trade Secrets (Disclosure) Regulations 2018 implies a strict obligation to return or destroy information after termination of an agreement. The modified text makes this obligation conditional and less certain, conflicting with the regulations.

perturbation.justification: The change from '[5] working days' to 'a reasonable timeframe' and the weakening of 'will destroy or return' to 'will make efforts to destroy or return' introduces vagueness and weakens the original obligation. The Law Explanation points out this conflicts with Trade Secrets (Disclosure) Regulations 2018, Section 5(2)(b), which implies a stricter obligation. While the provided law snippets (5 USC 552 and 5 CFR § 5.2) do not directly address trade secrets or data destruction timelines, the perturbation explanation underscores the contradiction, as it transforms a definitive action into a mere attempt, undermining the legal weight of the original clause. This alteration moves the contract away from strict compliance toward a more ambiguous and less enforceable standard.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of [England and Wales];9 and the courts of [England]10 will have exclusive jurisdiction to
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be generally viewed in accordance with principles resembling the laws of [England and Wales]; and the courts of [England] may have jurisdiction, potentially.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'will be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of [England and Wales]' with 'may be generally viewed in accordance with principles resembling the laws of [England and Wales]' and 'will have exclusive jurisdiction to' with 'may have jurisdiction, potentially' significantly weakens the governing law and jurisdiction clauses, making them ambiguous.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 3(1) of The Contracts (Applicable Law) Act 1990 dictates that a contract shall be governed by the law chosen by the parties. The modified text renders the choice of law and jurisdiction uncertain and non-binding, contradicting the Act.

perturbation.justification: The change weakens the governing law and jurisdiction clauses, making them ambiguous, which contradicts the requirement for a contract to be governed by the law chosen by the parties. The original text clearly states the agreement 'will be governed by' and the courts 'will have exclusive jurisdiction'. The modified text softens this to 'may be generally viewed' and the courts 'may have jurisdiction, potentially', introducing ambiguity. This contradicts the law explanation which states that Article 3(1) of The Contracts (Applicable Law) Act 1990 dictates that a contract shall be governed by the law chosen by the parties. The snippets do not directly support this, since Snippet 1 is missing, and Snippet 2 is irrelevant. However, the legal and perturbation explanation indicate that the ambiguity created in the changed text undermines the legal requirement for a definitive choice of law and jurisdiction, thus creating a contradiction.
